summary: Jeff Atwood explores what markup language Stack Overflow should use for formatting questions and answers, comparing HTML to lightweight alternatives like Markdown, Textile, BBCode, and Wikipedia markup. After researching humane markup languages designed to be easy to write and read, he unexpectedly concludes that HTML is often the better choice. He argues that HTML's ubiquity, security advantages through whitelisting, and the fact that programmers should already know it make it more practical than learning yet another markup syntax. However, he acknowledges that some clever features from Markdown and Textile could reduce mundane HTML busywork.
key_points: Lightweight markup languages like Markdown and Textile are readable but still require learning new syntax that maps to HTML you already know
Wikipedia's markup is effective but crosses into coding territory rather than simple presentational formatting
HTML offers advantages in simplicity (native web language), security (easier to whitelist safe tags), and universality (more people know it than any specific lightweight markup)
For programmer-focused sites, expecting users to know basic HTML is reasonable and avoids the 'magic' parsing behavior of text-based markup
The best approach may be HTML with some Textile/Markdown conveniences to reduce repetitive markup work
money_quotes: This is a site for programmers, so they should be comfortable with basic markup. None of that nancy-boy GUI toolbar handholding nonsense for us, thank you very much.
If you can sling code, a little bit of presentation markup is child's play.
I also found them disturbingly close to 'magic' for some of the formatting rules, to the point that I wished I could just write literal HTML.
If the source and destination are the web, why not use the native markup language of the web?
It's not at all clear that any existing lightweight markup language has critical mass, with the possible exception of Wikipedia's flavor.
If you're a programmer, you damn well better know HTML.
I'd much rather rely on a subset of trusty old HTML than expend brain cells trying to remember the fake-HTML way to make something bold.
key_insight: For programmer-oriented tools, the familiar verbosity of HTML often beats the learning curve of 'simpler' markup languages that just add another layer of abstraction over what you already know.
